title: Connecting Applications to Hybrid Tables
author: Adam Timm
universal_best_practices: Use bound variables (prepared statements) for all queries — enables plan cache reuse
Use connection pooling with long-lived connections — eliminates 100-500ms reconnect overhead
Use private key (JWT) authentication for service accounts
Colocate application in same cloud region as Snowflake account
Keep AUTOCOMMIT = TRUE (default) for OLTP workloads
Never benchmark HT latency via Snowsight — always use application driver

jdbc.pool: HikariCP — set maxLifetime and net.snowflake.jdbc.ttl to same value
jdbc.kafka_pattern: Kafka consumer (batched=true, concurrency=partitions) -> HikariCP pool -> executeBatch -> HT

python.binding_syntax: ? (qmark) — do NOT use f-strings or %s formatting
python.pool: SQLAlchemy create_engine with pool_size, pool_pre_ping=True, pool_recycle=1800

nodejs.binding_syntax: ? or :N positional — never string template literals
nodejs.pool_warning: Default evictionRunIntervalMillis=0 disables eviction — always set it

snowpark.bulk_pattern: session.sql('INSERT INTO ht SELECT ... FROM staging WHERE batch_id = ?').bind([id])

anti_patterns.name: String literals in queries
anti_patterns.impact: Disables plan cache, 10-100ms extra compilation per query

anti_patterns.name: Single-row inserts in a loop
anti_patterns.impact: N round-trips for N rows; use batch APIs instead

anti_patterns.name: Oversized connection pool
anti_patterns.impact: Warehouse queuing; match pool size to warehouse concurrency

anti_patterns.name: Benchmarking via Snowsight
anti_patterns.impact: 5-20x higher latency than driver; not representative

anti_patterns.name: Inconsistent schema qualification
anti_patterns.impact: Splits plan cache into multiple entries; reduces hit rate
